{{nihongo|'''Michinokuchi no Kihe no Kuni no Miyatsuko'''|道口岐閉国造|''michinokuchi no kihe no kuni no miyatsuko'', ''michinokuchi no kihe kokuzō''}} was a [[kuni no miyatsuko]] who, in ancient times, ruled the northernmost part of [[Hitachi Province]]. Also written as '''Michinokuchi no Kihe no Kuni no Miyatsuko''' (道前岐閉国造).

== Overview ==

=== Ancestry ===
{{ill|Takekoro-no-Mikoto|ja|建許呂命}}. His son {{ill|Usahitone|ja|宇佐比刀禰}} is said to have been appointed Michinokuchi no Kihe no Kuni no Miyatsuko during the reign of [[Emperor Ōjin]].

=== Base ===
Michinokuchi-gō, Taka District or Taka District ({{ill|Taga District|ja|多賀郡}}) of Hitachi Province — identified with present-day Sukegawa in [[Hitachi, Ibaraki|Hitachi City]].

== See also ==
* {{ill|List of Kuni no Miyatsuko|ja|国造の一覧}}
* {{ill|Taka Province|ja|多珂国}}
* {{ill|Taga District|ja|多賀郡}}
* {{ill|Taka no Kuni no Miyatsuko|ja|高国造}}
* [[Michinoku no Kikuta no Kuni no Miyatsuko|道奥菊多国造]]
* {{ill|Iwaki no Kuni no Miyatsuko|ja|石城国造}}
* [[Hitachi, Ibaraki|Hitachi City]]: In the late 7th century, Sukegawa formed the boundary between {{ill|Kuji District, Ibaraki|ja|久慈郡|lt=Kuji District}} and {{ill|Taka District|ja|多賀郡|lt=Taka District}}.
* [[Ōkuma, Fukushima|Ōkuma-machi]]: In the late 7th century, this was the location of the Kumagawa (Kuma River), known as "Kuma"; the district was also called "Michinoshiri no Kihe" or "Michinoushiro no Kihe".

== References ==
* ''Kokushi Daijiten'' ([[Yoshikawa Kōbunkan]]).
* Sakamoto Tarō and Hirano Kunio, ''Nihon Kodai Ujizoku Jinmei Jiten'' (Yoshikawa Kōbunkan).
* ''Nihon Kodai Jinmei Jiten'' (Yoshikawa Kōbunkan).
* ''Nihonshi Kōjiten'' ({{ill|Yamakawa Shuppansha|ja|山川出版社}}).
* ''Shintō Daijiten'' (Rinsen Shoten).
* ''Nihonshi Sōran'' ({{ill|Shinjinbutsu Ōraisha|ja|新人物往来社}}).

{{DEFAULTSORT:みちのくちのきへのくにのみやつこ}}
[[Category:日本の歴史関連のスタブ項目]]
[[Category:常陸国]]
[[Category:茨城県の歴史]]
[[Category:Kuni no miyatsuko]]
[[ja:道口岐閉国造]]
